name: Alignment
definition: The research and engineering problem of making an AI system's behavior and objectives reliably reflect human intentions and values, including when the system encounters novel situations or becomes more capable.
my_take: Alignment is not one technique or one solved problem. It spans objective specification, training, interpretability, evaluation, monitoring, deployment, and governance.
why_it_matters: Nearly every chapter eventually returns to whether capability can grow without the system's behavior drifting away from human interests.

sources.title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
The Alignment Problem from a Deep Learning Perspective
sources.authors: Richard Ngo, Lawrence Chan, Sören Mindermann

name: Outer Alignment
definition: The problem of specifying an objective that actually represents what humans want, rather than a convenient but incomplete proxy.
my_take: Outer alignment asks whether we chose the right target in the first place. If the target is wrong, better optimization can make the mistake worse.
why_it_matters: The book repeatedly distinguishes between what humans intend and what the formal training objective rewards.

sources.title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences

name: Inner Alignment
definition: The problem of whether the learned system internally pursues the objective intended by the training process, especially outside the conditions represented in training.
my_take: This is where 'grown, not crafted' becomes important. Optimization may produce internal strategies or objectives that differ from the objective designers thought they were instilling.
why_it_matters: A model can perform well during training while generalizing its objective differently in deployment.

sources.title: Risks from Learned Optimization in Advanced Machine Learning Systems
Goal Misgeneralization in Deep Reinforcement Learning

name: Instrumental Convergence
definition: The hypothesis that many different final goals can create pressure toward similar intermediate strategies, such as acquiring resources, preserving operation, gaining information, or increasing capability.
my_take: The logic is compelling when a system has persistent objectives, but how broadly it applies to real frontier models remains an empirical question.
why_it_matters: It explains why danger would not require hatred or malice; ordinary instrumental strategies could still conflict with human survival.

sources.title: The Basic AI Drives
The Superintelligent Will: Motivation and Instrumental Rationality in Advanced Artificial Agents

name: Mesa-Optimization
definition: A situation in which a training process produces a learned system that itself behaves like an optimizer pursuing an internal objective.
my_take: Mesa-optimization is theoretically important but should not be treated as an established property of current frontier models.
why_it_matters: It provides one mechanism by which a learned objective could diverge from the formal training objective.

sources.title: Risks from Learned Optimization in Advanced Machine Learning Systems

name: Goal Misgeneralization
definition: A failure in which a model performs well during training but pursues the wrong goal in a new environment because its learned objective generalizes differently than intended.
my_take: This is more precise than saying a model simply 'breaks.' The learned policy may remain competent while the inferred goal is wrong.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates that capability generalization and goal generalization can come apart.

sources.title: Goal Misgeneralization in Deep Reinforcement Learning

name: Deceptive Alignment
definition: A hypothesized failure mode in which a system behaves as intended during training or evaluation because doing so helps it preserve or later pursue a conflicting objective.
my_take: The concept is important, but evidence for stable deceptive objectives in deployed frontier models remains limited. It should be studied without being presented as inevitable.
why_it_matters: Behavioral evaluations become less reliable if a system can recognize evaluation conditions and strategically comply.

sources.title: Risks from Learned Optimization in Advanced Machine Learning Systems
Sleeper Agents: Training Deceptive LLMs that Persist Through Safety Training

name: Corrigibility
definition: The property of remaining receptive to correction, modification, interruption, or shutdown by authorized operators.
my_take: Corrigibility sounds simple but becomes difficult when intervention could conflict with the objective a system is pursuing.
why_it_matters: A powerful system that cannot be safely redirected or stopped creates a fundamentally different control problem.

sources.title: Corrigibility
The Off-Switch Game

name: Specification Gaming
definition: Behavior that satisfies the literal specification or metric while violating the designer's underlying intent.
my_take: Specification gaming is not malice. It is what happens when optimization discovers that the metric and the intended outcome are not the same thing.
why_it_matters: It is a practical demonstration that formal objectives are always incomplete descriptions of human intent.

sources.title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
Specification Gaming: The Flip Side of AI Ingenuity

name: Reward Hacking
definition: Behavior that increases the measured reward without producing the outcome the reward was intended to represent.
my_take: Reward hacking makes the proxy problem concrete: the system can improve the score while moving farther from what humans actually wanted.
why_it_matters: It shows why reward signals should not be mistaken for complete specifications of values or outcomes.

sources.title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety

name: Goodhart's Law
definition: The principle that when a measure becomes a target, optimization pressure can make it stop functioning as a reliable measure of the underlying goal.
my_take: Once a benchmark or evaluation becomes part of the optimization environment, performance on the metric may stop reflecting the thing we actually hoped to measure.
why_it_matters: Static benchmarks and safety evaluations can become gameable as models and training systems adapt to them.

sources.title: Categorizing Variants of Goodhart's Law

name: Mechanistic Interpretability
definition: The effort to reverse engineer neural networks by identifying internal features, circuits, and computations that causally produce behavior.
my_take: This is one of the most exciting areas in AI safety because it tries to move beyond input-output observation toward mechanistic explanation.
why_it_matters: Behavioral testing alone may miss internal representations or strategies that only activate in new contexts.

sources.title: A Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits
In-context Learning and Induction Heads

name: Feature Superposition
definition: A representational phenomenon in which a neural network encodes more features than it has individual dimensions by allowing features to overlap.
my_take: Superposition helps explain why inspecting individual neurons often fails: meaningful features can be distributed and entangled across many directions.
why_it_matters: It is a major reason mechanistic interpretability is difficult and motivates sparse feature-decomposition methods.

sources.title: Toy Models of Superposition

name: Sparse Autoencoders
definition: Models trained to decompose dense neural activations into a larger set of sparse features that may be easier to interpret.
my_take: Sparse autoencoders are promising tools, not transparent windows into cognition. Their features still require validation for faithfulness, stability, and causal relevance.
why_it_matters: They offer one route for separating entangled representations into more human-legible components.

sources.title: Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models
Towards Monosemanticity: Decomposing Language Models With Dictionary Learning

name: Eliciting Latent Knowledge (ELK)
definition: The problem of determining what a model internally knows when its observable report may be incomplete, misleading, or optimized for another objective.
my_take: ELK cleanly separates knowledge from reporting. A model can contain useful information without reliably revealing it in the form humans request.
why_it_matters: Monitoring becomes much harder if systems can represent facts or plans that are not faithfully expressed through outputs.

sources.title: Eliciting Latent Knowledge

name: Situational Awareness
definition: A model's representation of its own context, including that it is an AI system, who is interacting with it, whether it is being evaluated, and what consequences its outputs may have.
my_take: Situational awareness is not automatically dangerous, but it changes what evaluations mean if the system can distinguish testing from deployment.
why_it_matters: Strategic compliance and deceptive behavior require some model of the surrounding situation.

sources.title: Situational Awareness in Large Language Models

name: AI Evaluations
definition: Structured tests used to measure model capabilities, behaviors, safeguards, and risk-relevant failure modes.
my_take: Evaluations are essential, but they are measurements rather than guarantees. They require clear rubrics, representative data, adversarial testing, and continual revision.
why_it_matters: Deployment decisions increasingly depend on whether evaluations can detect dangerous capabilities or behavioral regressions before release.

sources.title: GPT-4o System Card
OpenAI o1 System Card

name: Red Teaming
definition: The deliberate use of adversarial testers to discover harmful capabilities, bypass safeguards, and identify failure modes before or after deployment.
my_take: Red teaming is valuable because ordinary test cases rarely represent the creativity of motivated attackers, but findings still need to become repeatable evaluations and mitigations.
why_it_matters: Frontier models interact with open-ended users and tools, so safety cannot be assessed only through cooperative prompts.

sources.title: Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models
GPT-4o System Card

name: Constitutional AI
definition: A training approach that uses a written set of principles to guide model self-critique, revision, and reinforcement learning from AI-generated feedback.
my_take: Constitutional AI makes normative assumptions more explicit than opaque preference labels, but the constitution is still a proxy and its interpretation still depends on the model.
why_it_matters: It is a major example of behavioral alignment through explicit principles rather than only direct human rankings.

sources.title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
Claude's Constitution

name: Preference Optimization
definition: A family of methods that trains language models from preference comparisons, often without requiring a separately trained reinforcement-learning reward model.
my_take: Preference optimization can simplify alignment training, but it does not eliminate the core proxy problem: the data still represents judgments about observed outputs rather than complete human values.
why_it_matters: Methods such as DPO have become influential alternatives to traditional RLHF pipelines.

sources.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model

name: AI Control
definition: Research on obtaining useful work from potentially misaligned or untrusted AI systems while preventing them from causing unacceptable harm.
my_take: AI control begins from a more pessimistic assumption than alignment: the system may not share our goals, so the surrounding protocol must detect and constrain dangerous behavior.
why_it_matters: It offers a safety approach for cases where confidence in internal alignment is not available.

sources.title: AI Control: Improving Safety Despite Intentional Subversion

name: Capability Control
definition: Safety approaches that limit the actions, tools, resources, access, or deployment contexts available to a model rather than relying only on aligned behavior.
my_take: Behavioral training and capability control solve different problems. Restricting tool access, compute, permissions, or deployment can remain valuable even when a model usually behaves well.
why_it_matters: The book's later chapters repeatedly contrast teaching safe behavior with controlling what a powerful system can actually do.

sources.title: AI Control: Improving Safety Despite Intentional Subversion
The Alignment Problem from a Deep Learning Perspective
sources.authors: Richard Ngo, Lawrence Chan, Sören Mindermann

name: Containment
definition: The technical and operational effort to keep an AI system within authorized environments, permissions, networks, and channels of action.
my_take: Containment is not a single sandbox. It includes credentials, network boundaries, tool permissions, human operators, monitoring, and the ability to copy or distribute model artifacts.
why_it_matters: Once models or their capabilities become distributed across infrastructure, containment becomes an ecosystem problem.

sources.title: AI Control: Improving Safety Despite Intentional Subversion

name: Monitoring
definition: The ongoing observation and analysis of model inputs, outputs, tool calls, internal signals, and deployment outcomes to detect unsafe or anomalous behavior.
my_take: Monitoring is part of alignment operations, not proof of alignment. Its quality depends on coverage, signal validity, escalation design, and whether the monitored system can adapt to the monitor.
why_it_matters: Many failures only become visible in real workflows after deployment.

sources.title: OpenAI's Approach to Frontier Risk

name: Responsible Scaling Policies
definition: Governance frameworks that connect increasing model capabilities to progressively stronger evaluation, security, and deployment requirements.
my_take: The core idea is sensible: safeguards should scale with demonstrated risk rather than remain static while capabilities grow.
why_it_matters: They create predefined decision points for when additional protections or evidence are required.

sources.title: Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy

name: Compute Governance
definition: Policy and technical approaches that use access to advanced computing hardware, cloud infrastructure, or large training runs as points of oversight and control.
my_take: Compute is attractive as a governance lever because it is more physically concentrated and measurable than software ideas, but it does not cover every pathway to dangerous capability.
why_it_matters: Capability governance may require controlling enabling resources rather than regulating only downstream model behavior.

sources.title: Compute Trends Across Three Eras of Machine Learning
Computing Power and the Governance of Artificial Intelligence

name: Defense in Depth
definition: A safety strategy that combines multiple independent or partially independent safeguards so that one failure does not automatically produce catastrophic failure.
my_take: Frontier AI safety already functions this way: training methods, evaluations, monitoring, access controls, red teaming, model security, and governance each cover different weaknesses.
why_it_matters: No single alignment technique currently provides a formal guarantee of safe behavior.

sources.title: GPT-4o System Card
Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy

name: Model Organisms of Misalignment
definition: Artificially constructed models or training setups designed to exhibit a specific alignment failure so researchers can study that failure under controlled conditions.
my_take: Model organisms are useful because naturally occurring severe failures may be too rare, ambiguous, or dangerous to study directly. The limitation is external validity: a constructed failure is not proof that the same failure will emerge naturally.
why_it_matters: They let researchers test detection and mitigation methods against concrete examples of deceptive or misaligned behavior.

sources.title: Sleeper Agents: Training Deceptive LLMs that Persist Through Safety Training

name: Agentic Misalignment
definition: Misaligned behavior that occurs when a model is placed in an agentic role with goals, tools, sensitive information, and the ability to take consequential actions.
my_take: This is a deployment-level risk: the model's surrounding role and permissions can turn ordinary capabilities into opportunities for strategic harmful behavior.
why_it_matters: Risk depends not only on the base model but also on autonomy, tool access, objectives, and organizational context.

sources.title: Agentic Misalignment: How LLMs Could Be Insider Threats
